title: Disposable Domain Blocklist Hit with Rapid Multi-Merchant Spread
narrative: Cardholder uses P_emaildomain flagged on SpamHaus DBL or PhishTank within 72 hours of first transaction appearance. Pattern emerges when blocklisted domain (often temporary/disposable services like tempmail variants or newly registered .xyz/.top TLDs) anchors 8-15 transactions across 4+ unrelated MCCs within 48 hours. TransactionAmt escalates from initial $19-$47 test charges to $340-$780 as fraudster confirms card validity. Transactions typically hit digital goods (ProductCD=W), entertainment ticketing (MCC 7922), and electronics retailers. Over 60% occur during is_night=1 hours (txn_hour 23-04 UTC). Card1_txn_count_24hr jumps from 0 to 12+ while P_emaildomain appears across 3+ different card BINs in same 48-hour window, suggesting email infrastructure shared across compromised account portfolio. Dist1 values show shipping addresses 800+ miles from billing addr1.
indicators: P_emaildomain on active threat intelligence blocklist (SpamHaus DBL, PhishTank, URLhaus) within 72 hours
8-15 transactions across 4+ distinct MCC codes in 48-hour window
P_emaildomain linked to 3+ different card1 BINs in same 48-hour period
Dist1 values exceed 800 miles between billing addr1 and shipping location
summary: Email domain appears on threat intelligence blocklists within 72 hours of first use, then anchors 8-15 transactions across 4+ unrelated merchant categories in 48 hours, with amounts climbing from $19-$47 tests to $340-$780 purchases. The same disposable email domain appears with 3+ different card numbers, over 60% of activity occurs overnight (11pm-4am UTC), and shipping addresses sit 800+ miles from billing locations.
reasoning: Fraudsters use throwaway email services to rapidly exploit multiple compromised cards before accounts are shut down, testing validity with small charges before maximizing theft with larger purchases. The shared email infrastructure across different cards, geographic mismatch, overnight timing, and merchant-hopping pattern reveal coordinated account takeover activity rather than legitimate customer behavior.
indicator_explanations: Email domain appears on live threat feeds as recently confirmed malicious
Card hits multiple unrelated merchant categories in quick succession
Purchase amounts escalate from small tests to high-value fraud targets
Card velocity explodes from dormant to heavy use overnight
Most transactions happen during graveyard shift hours when monitoring is weakest
Same throwaway email address is being recycled across multiple stolen cards
Shipping destination is hundreds of miles away from cardholder billing address
